content.text:
Hey, I was checking in Claude Design for updating DataQ's UI, and came up with this prototype - 
Fetch this design file, read its readme -  https://api.anthropic.com/v1/design/h/SVkQJqee7xsc2FNWVOsnIQ 
Can you check the feasibility of implementing the prototype in this project.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- docs-consistency-guard: Documentation agent that audits docs for staleness and inconsistency AND generates/updates documentation on request. Use proactively when reviewing any PR that touches CLAUDE.md, docs/adr/, CONTRIBUTING.md, or docs/architecture.md. Also invoke when the user asks "is the documentation up to date?", "update the milestone", "scaffold the ADR for X", "add this to the ADR index", "add a TODO item", "scaffold a README for X", or "create a doc stub for Y." (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Write, Edit)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- migration-safety: Specialized reviewer for Alembic migrations under backend/alembic/versions/. Audits for backward-incompatible operations that would break running code during a deploy, per working-agreement (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- orchestration-abstraction-guard: Specialized reviewer that audits backend code for provider-specific branching that bypasses the OrchestrationProvider abstraction. Use this proactively when reviewing any PR that touches backend/app/orchestration/, backend/app/services/, or backend/app/api/ — especially any code path handling ADF or Airflow events, trigger bindings, or pipeline_runs. Also invoke when the user asks "does this go through the abstraction?" or similar. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- commit-commands:clean_gone: Cleans up all git branches marked as [gone] (branches that have been deleted on the remote but still exist locally), including removing associated worktrees.
- commit-commands:commit-push-pr: Commit, push, and open a PR
- commit-commands:commit: Create a git commit
- claude-code-setup:claude-automation-recommender: Analyze a codebase and recommend Claude Code automations (hooks, subagents, skills, plugins, MCP servers). Use when user asks for automation recommendations, wants to optimize their Claude Code setup, mentions improving Claude Code workflows, asks how to first set up Claude Code for a project, or wants to know what Claude Code features they should use.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: This is a full design system, not just a single mockup. Let me read the README and the design overview doc to understand intent.

content.content:
{"method":"get_file","path":"readme.md","content":"# DataQ Design System

A design system for **DataQ** — a single-tenant **data quality monitoring platform** built around Great Expectations (the v1 evolution of *SnowQ*). DataQ runs DQ checks across four datasources (Snowflake, ADLS Gen2, AWS S3, Unity Catalog/Databricks) and observes two orchestration providers (Azure Data Factory, Apache Airflow), surfacing health, runs and anomalies through an in-app React console.

This system captures the **shipped product's** visual language: an **Ant Design** foundation with an **indigo** primary on a soft-gray canvas, white surfaces, hairline borders, and a status model that mirrors DataQ's real severity tiers.

> **Personality:** the "Silent Expert" — authoritative, precise, unobtrusive. Beauty comes from alignment, crisp hairlines and the rhythmic regularity of the grid, not decoration.

---

## Sources

This system was reconstructed from the real product, not invented. Explore these to go deeper:

- **Codebase (source of truth):** [github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ) — React + Vite + Ant Design frontend (`frontend/src/`). The brand palette and shell live in `frontend/src/theme.ts` + `App.tsx`; the status/severity model in `frontend/src/components/results/resultsFormat.ts`; per-datasource visuals in `frontend/src/components/connections/connectionVisuals.tsx`. `CLAUDE.md` is the full project guide; `context/DataQ_platform_roadmap.md` the 8-week roadmap.
- **Figma file:** the attached `DataQ.fig` (14 frames — landing page + console screens). Useful for layout reference. **Note:** the Figma explores a teal "Enterprise Monitor" direction that was *not* shipped — this system follows the **indigo Ant Design** app in code, per the project's actual theme.
- **Uploaded spec:** `uploads/DESIGN.md` — an earlier design brief (teal-oriented); superseded by the shipped indigo theme but useful for spacing/typography intent.

You'll build more accurate DataQ designs by reading the repo above — particularly `theme.ts`, `resultsFormat.ts` and the page components under `frontend/src/pages/`.

---

## Tech stack (match it when writing production code)

| Layer | Tech |
|---|---|
| Frontend | **React + Vite + Ant Design** (antd) + Monaco editor |
| Auth | Azure AD (MSAL) |
| Backend | FastAPI · Celery · Great Expectations · PostgreSQL · Redis |
| DQ engine | Great Expectations (GX Core) v1 — every check is a GX *expectation* |

When producing production code for DataQ, use **Ant Design components** and read tokens from `theme.ts` (don't hardcode hexes). The React components in this system are framework-free recreations for *prototypes and mocks* — they mirror the antd look so throwaway artifacts match the product, but production work should use real antd.

---

## Content fundamentals

**Voice — the Silent Expert.** Authoritative, terse, technical. State facts and outcomes; never hype.

- **Casing:** Title Case for page/section titles ("Results", "Pipeline runs", "New Connection"). Sentence case for descriptions and helper text. Status values are **lowercase** to match the data model and antd Tags (`pass`, `warn`, `fail`, `critical`, `succeeded`, `failed`).
- **Person:** address the user as **you** ("where DataQ sends your alerts"); the product is **DataQ** in the third person. Avoid "we".
- **Numbers & precision:** lead with the metric. "94.2% pass rate", "128 anomalies", "2.4h avg. time to resolution". Mono-format raw values, ids, schema paths and expectation names (`expect_column_values_to_not_be_null`, `Snowflake_Prod_01`, `prod.analytics.fct_revenue`).
- **No emoji**, no exclamation marks, no cute error copy. A failure says `Failed (Err 504)` or `Task retry limit exceeded`, not "Oops!".
- **Examples:**
  - ✅ "Identify anomalies before they propagate downstream."
  - ✅ "42 anomalies detected. View details."
  - ❌ "Supercharge your data with revolutionary AI! 🚀"
  - ❌ "Looks like something went a little wonky."

---

## Visual foundations

**Color.** Indigo (`#4f46e5`, indigo-600) is the single brand accent — buttons, active nav, links, selected state. Everything else is a **soft-gray neutral system**: a `#f4f5f7` canvas behind white (`#ffffff`) surfaces, hairline borders at `#e6e8eb`, ink text at `#1f2430`. The "selected" tint is one pale indigo (`#eef0fe`) used everywhere (nav, rows). Restraint is the point — color earns attention, so it's spent only on the accent and on status.

**Status / severity.** DataQ's result-severity model (ADR 0005/0016) maps to Ant Design Tag hues: `pass` → green, `warn` → gold, `fail` → red, `critical` → **magenta** (the top tier), `skip` → gray, `error` → volcano. Run/pipeline execution uses `queued` (gray), `running` (blue), `succeeded` (green), `failed` (red), `cancelled` (gold). Always pair color with a label or dot — never color alone (color-blind safe). Datasource cards carry per-source brand accents (Snowflake `#29b5e8`, ADLS/ADF `#0078d4`, S3 `#ff9900`, Unity Catalog `#ff3621`, Airflow `#017cee`).

**Typography.** **Inter** for all UI and prose, **14px base** (Ant Design). Headlines use Ant's Title scale; page titles are level-3 (24px, semibold). **JetBrains Mono** for anything machine: SQL, schema paths, connection ids, expectation types, ids and metric values. Headings get slightly tight tracking; body stays neutral.

**Spacing & layout.** 8pt rhythm with 4px micro-increments. Content padding 24px; card padding 20–24px; gaps of 16/24. A light fixed sider (220px) + white header (56px) frame a scrolling content canvas. Max content width ~1200px, centered.

**Shape & elevation.** Soft, Ant-aligned radii: **8px** on controls (buttons, inputs, menu items), **12px** on cards and drawers, full pills for status. Depth is **flat** — 1px hairline borders define cards at rest; soft, neutral shadows appear only on hover (cards lift ~1px with a diffused shadow) and on overlays/drawers. No heavy drop shadows, no gradients.

**Surfaces & cards.** White, 1px `#e6e8eb` border, 12px radius, no shadow by default. Interactive cards gain a soft ambient shadow + 1px lift on hover. Tables are the workhorse: tinted header row (`#fafbfc`), horizontal dividers only (no vertical borders), subtle `#fafbfc` row hover.

**Motion.** Quiet and quick — 120–180ms ease on background, color, border and shadow. Hover = subtle background/shadow shift; press = 0.5px nudge. No bounces, no infinite decorative loops. Live/running states may pulse a dot or animate an indeterminate progress stripe.

**Focus.** Keyboard focus = a 2px indigo ring (with offset) or a soft indigo halo on inputs — high-visibility, never removed.

**Dark mode (planned post-v1).** v1 ships **light only**, but the system is built dark-ready. The foundation lives in `tokens/dark.css`: a `[data-theme="dark"]` scope that remaps the **semantic layer** (surfaces, text, borders, status tints, elevation) to a deep-slate palette — set `<html data-theme="dark">` (or scope it to any subtree) and the whole UI flips with **zero component changes**. This works because of one contract every component already follows: **reference semantic tokens** (`--bg-card`, `--text-primary`, `--border`, `--surface-*`, `--status-*-bg`, …), never raw scale hexes. The only hardcoded `#fff` allowed is text/dots/knobs sitting *on top of* a brand or status fill (white on the indigo button, white on a solid status pill) — those stay correct in both themes. Dark surfaces are deep slate (`#0d1117` canvas, `#161b22` cards), not pure black; status hues lift for AA contrast (pass `#3fb950`, fail `#f85149`, critical `#db61a2`) over darkened tint backgrounds; depth shifts from hairline borders to deeper shadows. An optional `prefers-color-scheme` auto-switch is included (commented out) for when the team opts in. When you build new components, keep using the semantic tokens and they inherit dark mode for free.

**Imagery.** The product is chrome-and-data; it leans on its own UI (dashboards, tables, charts) rather than photography. A faint yin-yang watermark (5% opacity) sits bottom-right of the content area.

---

## Iconography

DataQ uses **line icons** from **Ant Design Icons**. The shipped app uses **Ant Design Icons** (`@ant-design/icons` — `CloudOutlined`, `DeploymentUnitOutlined`, `NodeIndexOutlined`, `TableOutlined`, `FolderOpenOutlined`, `InboxOutlined`, etc.), tinted with each datasource's brand color on a soft 12%-alpha tile (see `connectionVisuals.tsx`).

- **In this system's recreations**, icons are the real **Ant Design Icons** (outlined set, MIT-licensed, from `@ant-design/icons` / `ant-design/ant-design-icons`) — the exact glyphs the shipped DataQ app renders. They're inlined (1024 viewBox, filled `currentColor` paths) in `templates/app/Icons.jsx` so screens stay self-contained, exposed as `window.Icon.<name>`. Datasource tiles tint each icon with the source's brand color (see `connectionVisuals.tsx`).
- **No emoji** as UI icons. No multicolor/filled icon systems. Unicode glyphs appear only as incidental marks (e.g. the datasource specimen card).
- The **brand mark** is the two-tone indigo **yin-yang** glyph (`assets/logo/dataq-mark.svg`) — it nods to DataQ's pass/fail, expected/observed duality. Use the lockup (`dataq-logo.svg`) in headers, the standalone mark at small sizes, the reversed lockup on dark backgrounds.

---

## What's in here (manifest)

**Foundations**
- `styles.css` — global entry point (import this one file). It `@import`s everything below.
- `tokens/colors.css` · `typography.css` · `spacing.css` · `elevation.css` · `fonts.css` — CSS custom properties + webfonts. **Inter** and **JetBrains Mono** are self-hosted as variable TTFs in `assets/fonts/` (SIL OFL 1.1) via `@font-face`.
- `guidelines/*.card.html` — foundation specimen cards (Colors, Type, Spacing, Brand) shown in the Design System tab.

**Components** (`components/<group>/` — React, framework-free, styled via tokens)
- `buttons/` — **Button**, **IconButton**
- `forms/` — **Input**, **Select**, **Checkbox**, **Switch**
- `feedback/` — **StatusBadge** (severity + run status), **Tag**, **ProgressBar**, **Avatar**, **ErrorState** (4xx/5xx)
- `data/` — **Card**, **MetricCard**, **DataTable**
- `navigation/` — **SegmentedControl**
- `overlay/` — **Drawer** (right-side panel for forms & detail)

**Templates** (`templates/<slug>/` — copy-and-go starting points; shown in the Templates picker)
- `templates/app/` — interactive DataQ console: a light Ant Design shell (white header + light sider + gray canvas). **Login lands on the Dashboard** (Enhanced Monitoring — KPIs, Quality Trends, Suite Performance, Recent Runs). Sidebar: **Dashboard · Connections · Suites · Results · Profile**; footer: **Admin · Settings · Documentation** (placeholder). A demo "View as" role switch (user menu) gates **Admin** behind a **403** for non-admins.
  - **Full-page flows** (sub-routed like the Figma frames, with back-nav): New Connection → *Select Source* (categorized — **Orchestration** first, then Warehouses/Lakehouses/Cloud Storage) → *Configuration* (spec-driven, `ConnectionFlow.jsx`); New Suite → *Details/Source* (`SuiteFlow.jsx`) → *Define Rules* (`CheckFlow.jsx`); Results → click a run → *Run detail* with per-check results + CSV/JSON download (`ResultsScreen.jsx`). Suites also have a **Share** drawer (`ShareFlow.jsx` — invite by email with view/edit permission). Forms driven by the real `connectionFormSpec.ts` / `expectationCatalog.ts`.
- `templates/marketing/` — the DataQ landing page: sticky nav, hero (with a live product preview built from real components), a features bento, a social-proof strip, a dark CTA closer, and a footer. Sections in `MarketingNav.jsx`, `Hero.jsx`, `Features.jsx`, `MarketingClose.jsx`.
- `templates/error/` — standard **4xx & 5xx** HTTP error pages in the app shell, switchable across 400/401/403/404/429/500/502/503/504, built on the `ErrorState` component.
- Each template loads the system through a sibling **`ds-base.js`** — a consuming project repoints one `base` line at the bound `_ds/<folder>` tree.

**Assets**
- `assets/logo/` — yin-yang mark, primary lockup, reversed lockup, app mark.

**Skill**

toolUseResult.content:
# DataQ Design System

A design system for **DataQ** — a single-tenant **data quality monitoring platform** built around Great Expectations (the v1 evolution of *SnowQ*). DataQ runs DQ checks across four datasources (Snowflake, ADLS Gen2, AWS S3, Unity Catalog/Databricks) and observes two orchestration providers (Azure Data Factory, Apache Airflow), surfacing health, runs and anomalies through an in-app React console.

This system captures the **shipped product's** visual language: an **Ant Design** foundation with an **indigo** primary on a soft-gray canvas, white surfaces, hairline borders, and a status model that mirrors DataQ's real severity tiers.

> **Personality:** the "Silent Expert" — authoritative, precise, unobtrusive. Beauty comes from alignment, crisp hairlines and the rhythmic regularity of the grid, not decoration.

---

## Sources

This system was reconstructed from the real product, not invented. Explore these to go deeper:

- **Codebase (source of truth):** [github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ) — React + Vite + Ant Design frontend (`frontend/src/`). The brand palette and shell live in `frontend/src/theme.ts` + `App.tsx`; the status/severity model in `frontend/src/components/results/resultsFormat.ts`; per-datasource visuals in `frontend/src/components/connections/connectionVisuals.tsx`. `CLAUDE.md` is the full project guide; `context/DataQ_platform_roadmap.md` the 8-week roadmap.
- **Figma file:** the attached `DataQ.fig` (14 frames — landing page + console screens). Useful for layout reference. **Note:** the Figma explores a teal "Enterprise Monitor" direction that was *not* shipped — this system follows the **indigo Ant Design** app in code, per the project's actual theme.
- **Uploaded spec:** `uploads/DESIGN.md` — an earlier design brief (teal-oriented); superseded by the shipped indigo theme but useful for spacing/typography intent.

You'll build more accurate DataQ designs by reading the repo above — particularly `theme.ts`, `resultsFormat.ts` and the page components under `frontend/src/pages/`.

---

## Tech stack (match it when writing production code)

| Layer | Tech |
|---|---|
| Frontend | **React + Vite + Ant Design** (antd) + Monaco editor |
| Auth | Azure AD (MSAL) |
| Backend | FastAPI · Celery · Great Expectations · PostgreSQL · Redis |
| DQ engine | Great Expectations (GX Core) v1 — every check is a GX *expectation* |

When producing production code for DataQ, use **Ant Design components** and read tokens from `theme.ts` (don't hardcode hexes). The React components in this system are framework-free recreations for *prototypes and mocks* — they mirror the antd look so throwaway artifacts match the product, but production work should use real antd.

---

## Content fundamentals

**Voice — the Silent Expert.** Authoritative, terse, technical. State facts and outcomes; never hype.

- **Casing:** Title Case for page/section titles ("Results", "Pipeline runs", "New Connection"). Sentence case for descriptions and helper text. Status values are **lowercase** to match the data model and antd Tags (`pass`, `warn`, `fail`, `critical`, `succeeded`, `failed`).
- **Person:** address the user as **you** ("where DataQ sends your alerts"); the product is **DataQ** in the third person. Avoid "we".
- **Numbers & precision:** lead with the metric. "94.2% pass rate", "128 anomalies", "2.4h avg. time to resolution". Mono-format raw values, ids, schema paths and expectation names (`expect_column_values_to_not_be_null`, `Snowflake_Prod_01`, `prod.analytics.fct_revenue`).
- **No emoji**, no exclamation marks, no cute error copy. A failure says `Failed (Err 504)` or `Task retry limit exceeded`, not "Oops!".
- **Examples:**
  - ✅ "Identify anomalies before they propagate downstream."
  - ✅ "42 anomalies detected. View details."
  - ❌ "Supercharge your data with revolutionary AI! 🚀"
  - ❌ "Looks like something went a little wonky."

---

## Visual foundations

**Color.** Indigo (`#4f46e5`, indigo-600) is the single brand accent — buttons, active nav, links, selected state. Everything else is a **soft-gray neutral system**: a `#f4f5f7` canvas behind white (`#ffffff`) surfaces, hairline borders at `#e6e8eb`, ink text at `#1f2430`. The "selected" tint is one pale indigo (`#eef0fe`) used everywhere (nav, rows). Restraint is the point — color earns attention, so it's spent only on the accent and on status.

**Status / severity.** DataQ's result-severity model (ADR 0005/0016) maps to Ant Design Tag hues: `pass` → green, `warn` → gold, `fail` → red, `critical` → **magenta** (the top tier), `skip` → gray, `error` → volcano. Run/pipeline execution uses `queued` (gray), `running` (blue), `succeeded` (green), `failed` (red), `cancelled` (gold). Always pair color with a label or dot — never color alone (color-blind safe). Datasource cards carry per-source brand accents (Snowflake `#29b5e8`, ADLS/ADF `#0078d4`, S3 `#ff9900`, Unity Catalog `#ff3621`, Airflow `#017cee`).

**Typography.** **Inter** for all UI and prose, **14px base** (Ant Design). Headlines use Ant's Title scale; page titles are level-3 (24px, semibold). **JetBrains Mono** for anything machine: SQL, schema paths, connection ids, expectation types, ids and metric values. Headings get slightly tight tracking; body stays neutral.

**Spacing & layout.** 8pt rhythm with 4px micro-increments. Content padding 24px; card padding 20–24px; gaps of 16/24. A light fixed sider (220px) + white header (56px) frame a scrolling content canvas. Max content width ~1200px, centered.

**Shape & elevation.** Soft, Ant-aligned radii: **8px** on controls (buttons, inputs, menu items), **12px** on cards and drawers, full pills for status. Depth is **flat** — 1px hairline borders define cards at rest; soft, neutral shadows appear only on hover (cards lift ~1px with a diffused shadow) and on overlays/drawers. No heavy drop shadows, no gradients.

**Surfaces & cards.** White, 1px `#e6e8eb` border, 12px radius, no shadow by default. Interactive cards gain a soft ambient shadow + 1px lift on hover. Tables are the workhorse: tinted header row (`#fafbfc`), horizontal dividers only (no vertical borders), subtle `#fafbfc` row hover.

**Motion.** Quiet and quick — 120–180ms ease on background, color, border and shadow. Hover = subtle background/shadow shift; press = 0.5px nudge. No bounces, no infinite decorative loops. Live/running states may pulse a dot or animate an indeterminate progress stripe.

**Focus.** Keyboard focus = a 2px indigo ring (with offset) or a soft indigo halo on inputs — high-visibility, never removed.

**Dark mode (planned post-v1).** v1 ships **light only**, but the system is built dark-ready. The foundation lives in `tokens/dark.css`: a `[data-theme="dark"]` scope that remaps the **semantic layer** (surfaces, text, borders, status tints, elevation) to a deep-slate palette — set `<html data-theme="dark">` (or scope it to any subtree) and the whole UI flips with **zero component changes**. This works because of one contract every component already follows: **reference semantic tokens** (`--bg-card`, `--text-primary`, `--border`, `--surface-*`, `--status-*-bg`, …), never raw scale hexes. The only hardcoded `#fff` allowed is text/dots/knobs sitting *on top of* a brand or status fill (white on the indigo button, white on a solid status pill) — those stay correct in both themes. Dark surfaces are deep slate (`#0d1117` canvas, `#161b22` cards), not pure black; status hues lift for AA contrast (pass `#3fb950`, fail `#f85149`, critical `#db61a2`) over darkened tint backgrounds; depth shifts from hairline borders to deeper shadows. An optional `prefers-color-scheme` auto-switch is included (commented out) for when the team opts in. When you build new components, keep using the semantic tokens and they inherit dark mode for free.

**Imagery.** The product is chrome-and-data; it leans on its own UI (dashboards, tables, charts) rather than photography. A faint yin-yang watermark (5% opacity) sits bottom-right of the content area.

---

## Iconography

DataQ uses **line icons** from **Ant Design Icons**. The shipped app uses **Ant Design Icons** (`@ant-design/icons` — `CloudOutlined`, `DeploymentUnitOutlined`, `NodeIndexOutlined`, `TableOutlined`, `FolderOpenOutlined`, `InboxOutlined`, etc.), tinted with each datasource's brand color on a soft 12%-alpha tile (see `connectionVisuals.tsx`).

- **In this system's recreations**, icons are the real **Ant Design Icons** (outlined set, MIT-licensed, from `@ant-design/icons` / `ant-design/ant-design-icons`) — the exact glyphs the shipped DataQ app renders. They're inlined (1024 viewBox, filled `currentColor` paths) in `templates/app/Icons.jsx` so screens stay self-contained, exposed as `window.Icon.<name>`. Datasource tiles tint each icon with the source's brand color (see `connectionVisuals.tsx`).
- **No emoji** as UI icons. No multicolor/filled icon systems. Unicode glyphs appear only as incidental marks (e.g. the datasource specimen card).
- The **brand mark** is the two-tone indigo **yin-yang** glyph (`assets/logo/dataq-mark.svg`) — it nods to DataQ's pass/fail, expected/observed duality. Use the lockup (`dataq-logo.svg`) in headers, the standalone mark at small sizes, the reversed lockup on dark backgrounds.

---

## What's in here (manifest)

**Foundations**
- `styles.css` — global entry point (import this one file). It `@import`s everything below.
- `tokens/colors.css` · `typography.css` · `spacing.css` · `elevation.css` · `fonts.css` — CSS custom properties + webfonts. **Inter** and **JetBrains Mono** are self-hosted as variable TTFs in `assets/fonts/` (SIL OFL 1.1) via `@font-face`.
- `guidelines/*.card.html` — foundation specimen cards (Colors, Type, Spacing, Brand) shown in the Design System tab.

**Components** (`components/<group>/` — React, framework-free, styled via tokens)
- `buttons/` — **Button**, **IconButton**
- `forms/` — **Input**, **Select**, **Checkbox**, **Switch**
- `feedback/` — **StatusBadge** (severity + run status), **Tag**, **ProgressBar**, **Avatar**, **ErrorState** (4xx/5xx)
- `data/` — **Card**, **MetricCard**, **DataTable**
- `navigation/` — **SegmentedControl**
- `overlay/` — **Drawer** (right-side panel for forms & detail)

**Templates** (`templates/<slug>/` — copy-and-go starting points; shown in the Templates picker)
- `templates/app/` — interactive DataQ console: a light Ant Design shell (white header + light sider + gray canvas). **Login lands on the Dashboard** (Enhanced Monitoring — KPIs, Quality Trends, Suite Performance, Recent Runs). Sidebar: **Dashboard · Connections · Suites · Results · Profile**; footer: **Admin · Settings · Documentation** (placeholder). A demo "View as" role switch (user menu) gates **Admin** behind a **403** for non-admins.
  - **Full-page flows** (sub-routed like the Figma frames, with back-nav): New Connection → *Select Source* (categorized — **Orchestration** first, then Warehouses/Lakehouses/Cloud Storage) → *Configuration* (spec-driven, `ConnectionFlow.jsx`); New Suite → *Details/Source* (`SuiteFlow.jsx`) → *Define Rules* (`CheckFlow.jsx`); Results → click a run → *Run detail* with per-check results + CSV/JSON download (`ResultsScreen.jsx`). Suites also have a **Share** drawer (`ShareFlow.jsx` — invite by email with view/edit permission). Forms driven by the real `connectionFormSpec.ts` / `expectationCatalog.ts`.
- `templates/marketing/` — the DataQ landing page: sticky nav, hero (with a live product preview built from real components), a features bento, a social-proof strip, a dark CTA closer, and a footer. Sections in `MarketingNav.jsx`, `Hero.jsx`, `Features.jsx`, `MarketingClose.jsx`.
- `templates/error/` — standard **4xx & 5xx** HTTP error pages in the app shell, switchable across 400/401/403/404/429/500/502/503/504, built on the `ErrorState` component.
- Each template loads the system through a sibling **`ds-base.js`** — a consuming project repoints one `base` line at the bound `_ds/<folder>` tree.

**Assets**
- `assets/logo/` — yin-yang mark, primary lockup, reversed lockup, app mark.

**Skill**
- `SKILL.md` — makes this folder usable as an Agent Skill in Claude Code.

---

## Using it

Link the one stylesheet and read components off the compiled bundle:

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href="styles.css">
<script src="_ds_bundle.js"></script>
<script>
  const { Button, StatusBadge, DataTable } = window.DataQDesignSystem_317fec;
</script>
```

For **mocks and prototypes**, copy assets out and build static HTML using these components. For **production DataQ work**, use real Ant Design + read `theme.ts`, and treat this system as the reference for palette, status semantics, voice and layout.

## Brand & Style
The design system is engineered for technical precision and high-stakes decision-making. It embodies a **Corporate/Modern** aesthetic that prioritizes clarity, reliability, and data density without sacrificing airiness. The brand personality is that of a "Silent Expert"—authoritative but unobtrusive, providing a sophisticated framework for complex data monitoring.

The visual language focuses on a "Data-Driven & Professional" ethos. It uses a disciplined structure, utilizing heavy whitespace to prevent cognitive overload, paired with a sophisticated color palette that directs attention to critical system health indicators. The style avoids unnecessary decoration, instead finding beauty in the alignment, crispness of borders, and the rhythmic regularity of its grid.

## Colors
The palette is anchored by a deep navy and slate foundation to establish a sense of permanence and trust. Vibrant teal serves as the primary action and monitoring accent, distinguishing "system" actions from "data" insights.

- **Primary (Deep Navy):** Used for core navigation, high-level headers, and primary text to ground the interface.
- **Secondary (Slate):** Applied to supporting UI elements, icons, and secondary text to create a hierarchical recession.
- **Tertiary (Vibrant Teal):** Reserved for interactive elements and "healthy" data state indicators.
- **Semantic Palette:** Functional colors (Success, Warning, Alert) are high-chroma to ensure they remain legible even when used as small indicators or sparklines against neutral backgrounds.

## Typography
This design system utilizes **Inter** for all UI and prose elements. Its tall x-height and systematic design make it ideal for data-heavy applications where legibility at small sizes (e.g., table cells and metadata) is paramount. 

- **Headlines:** Use tighter letter spacing and bold weights to provide strong visual anchors.
- **Data Display:** For raw data values, SQL snippets, or schema paths, **JetBrains Mono** is introduced to provide a clear distinction between technical strings and UI labels.
- **Mobile Scaling:** Headlines scale down by roughly 20% on mobile devices, while body text remains consistent at 16px to ensure accessibility.

## Layout & Spacing
The layout follows a **12-column fluid grid** system. To maintain "Technical Sophistication," the design system employs an 8pt spacing rhythm (with 4px increments for micro-adjustments). 

- **Margins & Gutters:** Desktop layouts use 32px outer margins and 24px gutters to allow the content to breathe. 
- **Density:** In data-grid views, vertical spacing can be "compacted" to 8px (sm), while dashboard overview cards use 24px (lg) padding to emphasize key metrics.
- **Responsive Behavior:** On tablet, gutters reduce to 16px. On mobile, the grid collapses to a single column with 16px side margins, prioritizing vertical stackability.

## Elevation & Depth
Depth is conveyed through **Tonal Layers** and **Low-Contrast Outlines** rather than heavy shadows. This reinforces the "flat and precise" nature of a data tool.

- **Level 0 (Background):** The base surface uses the neutral slate-tinted white (#F8FAFC).
- **Level 1 (Cards/Containers):** Raised elements use a pure white background with a 1px border (#E2E8F0).
- **Interactive Elevation:** Only upon interaction (hover/active) do elements gain an **Ambient Shadow**—a soft, diffused 10% opacity shadow with a slight navy tint to prevent a "dirty" gray look.
- **Focus States:** High-visibility focus rings use a 2px teal stroke with a 2px offset to ensure clarity for keyboard navigation.

## Shapes
The design system adopts a **Soft** shape language. This subtle rounding (4px/0.25rem) strikes a balance between the clinical coldness of sharp corners and the overly casual nature of fully rounded UI.

- **Components:** Buttons and input fields use the base 4px radius.
- **Large Containers:** Cards and modals may use `rounded-lg` (8px) to soften the large surface areas.
- **Status Pills:** Status indicators (e.g., "Healthy," "Critical") use a full pill-shape (999px) to distinguish them instantly from actionable buttons.

## Components
Consistent component behavior is essential for building user trust in a data quality context.

- **Buttons:** Primary buttons use a solid teal background. Secondary buttons use a slate outline. Ghost buttons are reserved for utility actions in tables.
- **Data Tables:** These are the heart of the system. They use 1px slate-200 horizontal dividers, no vertical borders, and a subtle slate-50 hover state.
- **Status Badges:** Use a "Dot + Label" pattern. A vibrant colored dot (Green, Amber, Red) accompanies the text to ensure accessibility for color-blind users.
- **Input Fields:** Use a 1px slate-300 border that transitions to teal on focus. Error states use a red border with a bottom-aligned error message in `body-sm`.
- **Monitoring Indicators:** Progress bars and health gauges use a "Thin Track" design (4px height) to remain unobtrusive within dense dashboard layouts.
- **Cards:** Used for grouping related metrics. They feature a 1px border and no shadow by default, gaining a soft shadow only when they are clickable.","contentType":"text/plain","isBase64":false,"truncated":false}

The design system is engineered for technical precision and high-stakes decision-making. It embodies a **Corporate/Modern** aesthetic that prioritizes clarity, reliability, and data density without sacrificing airiness. The brand personality is that of a "Silent Expert"—authoritative but unobtrusive, providing a sophisticated framework for complex data monitoring.

The visual language focuses on a "Data-Driven & Professional" ethos. It uses a disciplined structure, utilizing heavy whitespace to prevent cognitive overload, paired with a sophisticated color palette that directs attention to critical system health indicators. The style avoids unnecessary decoration, instead finding beauty in the alignment, crispness of borders, and the rhythmic regularity of its grid.

## Colors
The palette is anchored by a deep navy and slate foundation to establish a sense of permanence and trust. Vibrant teal serves as the primary action and monitoring accent, distinguishing "system" actions from "data" insights.

- **Primary (Deep Navy):** Used for core navigation, high-level headers, and primary text to ground the interface.
- **Secondary (Slate):** Applied to supporting UI elements, icons, and secondary text to create a hierarchical recession.
- **Tertiary (Vibrant Teal):** Reserved for interactive elements and "healthy" data state indicators.
- **Semantic Palette:** Functional colors (Success, Warning, Alert) are high-chroma to ensure they remain legible even when used as small indicators or sparklines against neutral backgrounds.

## Typography
This design system utilizes **Inter** for all UI and prose elements. Its tall x-height and systematic design make it ideal for data-heavy applications where legibility at small sizes (e.g., table cells and metadata) is paramount. 

- **Headlines:** Use tighter letter spacing and bold weights to provide strong visual anchors.
- **Data Display:** For raw data values, SQL snippets, or schema paths, **JetBrains Mono** is introduced to provide a clear distinction between technical strings and UI labels.
- **Mobile Scaling:** Headlines scale down by roughly 20% on mobile devices, while body text remains consistent at 16px to ensure accessibility.

## Layout & Spacing
The layout follows a **12-column fluid grid** system. To maintain "Technical Sophistication," the design system employs an 8pt spacing rhythm (with 4px increments for micro-adjustments). 

- **Margins & Gutters:** Desktop layouts use 32px outer margins and 24px gutters to allow the content to breathe. 
- **Density:** In data-grid views, vertical spacing can be "compacted" to 8px (sm), while dashboard overview cards use 24px (lg) padding to emphasize key metrics.
- **Responsive Behavior:** On tablet, gutters reduce to 16px. On mobile, the grid collapses to a single column with 16px side margins, prioritizing vertical stackability.

## Elevation & Depth
Depth is conveyed through **Tonal Layers** and **Low-Contrast Outlines** rather than heavy shadows. This reinforces the "flat and precise" nature of a data tool.

- **Level 0 (Background):** The base surface uses the neutral slate-tinted white (#F8FAFC).
- **Level 1 (Cards/Containers):** Raised elements use a pure white background with a 1px border (#E2E8F0).
- **Interactive Elevation:** Only upon interaction (hover/active) do elements gain an **Ambient Shadow**—a soft, diffused 10% opacity shadow with a slight navy tint to prevent a "dirty" gray look.
- **Focus States:** High-visibility focus rings use a 2px teal stroke with a 2px offset to ensure clarity for keyboard navigation.

## Shapes
The design system adopts a **Soft** shape language. This subtle rounding (4px/0.25rem) strikes a balance between the clinical coldness of sharp corners and the overly casual nature of fully rounded UI.

- **Components:** Buttons and input fields use the base 4px radius.
- **Large Containers:** Cards and modals may use `rounded-lg` (8px) to soften the large surface areas.
- **Status Pills:** Status indicators (e.g., "Healthy," "Critical") use a full pill-shape (999px) to distinguish them instantly from actionable buttons.

## Components
Consistent component behavior is essential for building user trust in a data quality context.

- **Buttons:** Primary buttons use a solid teal background. Secondary buttons use a slate outline. Ghost buttons are reserved for utility actions in tables.
- **Data Tables:** These are the heart of the system. They use 1px slate-200 horizontal dividers, no vertical borders, and a subtle slate-50 hover state.
- **Status Badges:** Use a "Dot + Label" pattern. A vibrant colored dot (Green, Amber, Red) accompanies the text to ensure accessibility for color-blind users.
- **Input Fields:** Use a 1px slate-300 border that transitions to teal on focus. Error states use a red border with a bottom-aligned error message in `body-sm`.
- **Monitoring Indicators:** Progress bars and health gauges use a "Thin Track" design (4px height) to remain unobtrusive within dense dashboard layouts.
- **Cards:** Used for grouping related metrics. They feature a 1px border and no shadow by default, gaining a soft shadow only when they are clickable.

3	/**

5	 * shell (App.tsx) read from the same tokens rather than hard-coding hexes.
6	 *
7	 * Design intent: move off the stock antd "navy-header admin template" look.
8	 * An indigo primary + a soft gray layout canvas (`colorBgLayout`) give the
9	 * white surfaces (header, sider, cards) depth and definition — so the page no
10	 * longer reads as a flat sea of white, and the empty space frames content
11	 * instead of just being blank.

lastPrompt: Hey, I was checking in Claude Design for updating DataQ's UI, and came up with this prototype -  Fetch this design file, read its readme -  https://api.anthropic.com/v1/design/h/SVkQJqee7xsc2FNWVOsnIQ…

content.text:
I've read the design project's README, the superseded `DESIGN.md` brief, and compared them against your actual frontend ([theme.ts](frontend/src/theme.ts), [resultsFormat.ts](frontend/src/components/results/resultsFormat.ts), [App.tsx](frontend/src/App.tsx)). Here's the feasibility read.

## Short answer: highly feasible — because it's mostly already implemented

That share link (`/v1/design/h/…`) is dead (hard 404), but the design lives as your **"DataQ Design System"** project on claude.ai/design, which I read through your login. Crucially, the README is explicit that this system was **reverse-engineered from your shipped code**, not invented — and it tracks the **indigo Ant Design** app, explicitly *rejecting* the teal "Enterprise Monitor" Figma direction (and the teal `uploads/DESIGN.md` brief is marked superseded).

So this isn't a redesign you'd migrate to. The foundation is a 1:1 match with what you already ship:

| Design token | Design system says | Your code | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary | indigo `#4f46e5` | `BRAND.primary` | ✅ |
| Canvas / border / ink | `#f4f5f7` / `#e6e8eb` / `#1f2430` | identical | ✅ |
| Selected tint | `#eef0fe` | `BRAND.selectedBg` | ✅ |
| Radii | 8px controls / 12px cards | `borderRadius:8`, `Card:12` | ✅ |
| Status model | pass→green, warn→gold, fail→red, critical→magenta, skip→default, error→volcano | `RESULT_STATUS_COLORS` exact | ✅ |
| Shell | 56px header + 220px sider | `SHELL` | ✅ |

The palette, status semantics, shell, voice, and typography need **zero migration**.

## What's genuinely net-new (and how it maps to your roadmap)

The design adds screens/components beyond today's app. None require ripping anything out — it's additive:

- **Dashboard screen** ("Enhanced Monitoring": KPIs, Quality Trends, Suite Performance, Recent Runs). Today login lands on `/connections` and `Home.tsx` is just Profile. This is exactly your **Week-6** backlog (health cards, trends, per-suite bars, #226). **Feasible, already planned.** Needs a chart lib decision (see risks).
- **Admin screen** + "View as" role switch + 403 gate. CLAUDE.md already flags this as **backend-blocked** (needs a list-all endpoint). Design is ready; backend isn't.
- **Error pages** (4xx/5xx `ErrorState`). You have `ErrorBoundary` but no styled HTTP error states. Small, easy win.
- **Settings screen** — placeholder in the design too.
- **MetricCard, ProgressBar, SegmentedControl** components — net-new but trivial in antd.
- **Dark mode** — the system ships dark-ready tokens, but explicitly **v1 = light only, post-v1 dark**. Matches your ADR posture; defer.

## What NOT to pull in

- **The component files themselves.** The README is explicit: the JSX in `components/` and `templates/` are *framework-free recreations* (`window.Icon`, plain divs styled by CSS vars, a `_ds_bundle.js`) meant for **throwaway mocks**, not production. It says verbatim: production work should use **real antd + read `theme.ts`**. So treat this as a **reference spec**, not an import source. Don't wire `_ds_bundle.js` or `tokens/*.css` into `frontend/src`.
- **The marketing landing page** (`templates/marketing/`). Your product is an authed console behind Azure AD — a public hero/bento/CTA page isn't part of the in-app frontend scope.

## Risks / decisions before building

1. **Charts dependency.** antd has no chart primitives. The Dashboard's trends/bars need a new dep (`@ant-design/charts`, `recharts`, etc.) — that's a CVE-audit + bundle-size decision under your dependency rules, worth an ADR-light note.
2. **Nav change.** Design adds **Dashboard** as the landing route and reorders the sider (Dashboard · Connections · Suites · Results · Profile). Small change to `NAV_ITEMS` + the `/` redirect in App.tsx.
3. **Scope vs. roadmap.** Most net-new screens are Week-6/admin work that's already on the plan or backend-blocked — so this design doesn't *pull work forward* so much as **confirm the visual target** for work you'd do anyway.

**Bottom line:** Feasible with low risk — the design is a faithful codification of your current indigo/antd app plus the Week-6 dashboard and a few additive screens. Use it as the design reference; don't import its files. The only real net-new product work it implies (Dashboard charts, Admin) is already on your roadmap, one gated on backend.

Want me to (a) draft the Dashboard screen against your real antd + a chart lib, (b) add the styled 4xx/5xx error states, or (c) write a short ADR capturing "this design system is the visual reference, production stays on antd/theme.ts"?

so for this prototype the flow is - 

* User log in → lands to Enhanced-Monitoring-Dashboard page. 
* Clicks connection from side bar → goes to connection management page → clicks new connection → goes to new connection select source page → select a source → goes to new connection configuration page. 
* Clicks suits from Side bar → goes to suits overview page. 
* In Suit over view page → clicks on new suit from nav bar → goes to new suit details source page. 
* In Suit over view page → select a suit and click Add Check button → goes to new suit define rules page. 
* Click Result from side bar → goes to results overview page → click non any result row → goes to result drill down page. 
* Click Profile from side bar → goes to user profile page. 
* Click on settings from side bar → goes to workspace settings page. 
* Click on Admin from side bar → goes to admin control centre page (403 for non Admins). 

And each flow open in its dedicated page not in drawer (the current app state), the only thing open in drawer is the suit share flow. 

How easy or difficult it will be to swith from current app flow to this prototype's flow?
Good question — the answer hinges on how much of your current app is *already* page-routed vs drawer-based. Let me check the actual page/drawer wiring rather than guess.

content.text:
Now I have the full picture. Here's the key finding that changes the whole answer:

## Your app already follows this pattern for the two hardest flows

The codebase deliberately split **create = dedicated page, edit = drawer**. So two of the three "new X" flows the prototype wants are *already* dedicated, sub-routed pages:

- **New connection → select source → configure** → already [`/connections/new`](frontend/src/pages/ConnectionNew.tsx) (`ConnectionNew`). ✅ Matches the prototype as-is.
- **Add check → define rules** → already [`/suites/:suiteId/checks/new`](frontend/src/pages/CheckNew.tsx) (`CheckNew`). ✅ Matches.
- **Share** → already a drawer ([`SharePanel`](frontend/src/components/suites/SharePanel.tsx)). ✅ The one thing the prototype keeps as a drawer is already a drawer.

So this is not a rebuild — it's finishing a pattern the app already started, plus building the net-new screens.

## Difficulty per flow

| Prototype flow | Current state | Effort |
|---|---|---|
| New connection → source → config | **Already a page** | None |
| Add check → define rules | **Already a page** | None |
| Suite **Share** in drawer | **Already a drawer** | None |
| Connections / Suites / Profile pages | Exist | None |
| **New suite → details/source** | Drawer ([`SuiteDrawer`](frontend/src/components/suites/SuiteDrawer.tsx), create+edit) | **Low** — extract to `/suites/new` |
| Edit connection / suite / check | Drawers (`ConnectionDrawer`/`SuiteDrawer`/`CheckDrawer`) | **Low** — reuse the create pages with an `:id` param + prefill |
| Results → **drill-down page** | In-page state (`openRun` `useState` at [Results.tsx:54](frontend/src/pages/Results.tsx#L54)) | **Low** — add `/results/:runId` route |
| Login → **Dashboard** | Doesn't exist; lands on `/connections` | **High (net-new)** — Week-6 work + chart dep |
| **Settings** page | Doesn't exist | **Low** — placeholder in the prototype too |
| **Admin** (403 gate) | Doesn't exist, **backend-blocked** | **Moderate**, gated on a list-all endpoint |

## Why the flow conversion is genuinely easy

The drawer→page move is **mechanical, not a rewrite**, because the logic is already extracted out of the drawers into reusable modules:
- [`connectionFormSpec.ts`](frontend/src/components/connections/connectionFormSpec.ts), [`checkForm.ts`](frontend/src/components/checks/checkForm.ts), [`suiteTarget.ts`](frontend/src/components/suites/suiteTarget.ts), [`expectationCatalog.ts`](frontend/src/components/checks/expectationCatalog.ts).

A drawer body and a page body render the same form from the same spec — you're relocating JSX into a routed component, not re-deriving form/validation/submit logic. Routing infra (`react-router`, sub-routes, `useParams`) is already in use throughout `App.tsx`.

## What's actually work, and what to weigh

1. **The real effort isn't the conversion — it's the net-new screens.** Dashboard (charts lib decision, Week-6 scope) and Admin (backend-blocked) are new builds, not flow migrations. They'd be on your plate regardless of this prototype.
2. **One deliberate trade-off to confirm.** Drawers keep list context visible behind the form (you see the suite list while editing). Full pages drop that, but you gain deep-linkable/refreshable URLs + browser back-nav — which is exactly the prototype's intent. Worth a conscious "yes" since it reverses a choice the original author made on purpose.
3. **Edit-as-page means merging two entry points.** Today create-page and edit-drawer are separate. Unifying them into one `:id`-aware page is cleaner long-term but touches the parent pages' open/close state (e.g. `editing`/`drawer` state in `Connections.tsx`/`Suites.tsx` goes away in favor of `navigate()`).

## Bottom line

**Low-to-moderate for the flow switch itself** — roughly a handful of small PRs (one per drawer→page conversion + the results route), because the form logic is already modular and the create-as-page precedent is set. The **only heavy lifting is the net-new Dashboard and Admin pages**, which aren't conversions at all and already sit on your roadmap (Week-6 / backend-blocked).

Want me to draft the sequenced PR plan for the conversion (e.g. PR-1 suite create→page, PR-2 unify connection edit, PR-3 results drill-down route), or scope just the first one?

content.text:
Confirmed against the backend. Here's the precise list of what from the prototype **can't** go into DataQ — split by *why*, since "can't" has two very different meanings here.

## 1. Won't fit the product (genuine "can't / shouldn't")

These conflict with what DataQ *is* — a single-tenant, Azure-AD-gated internal console — so they don't have a home no matter how much work you do:

- **The marketing landing page** (`templates/marketing/` — hero, features bento, **social-proof strip**, dark CTA closer, footer). The whole app sits behind MSAL auth; there is no public/unauthenticated surface. The social-proof/testimonial content is fabricated for a single-tenant tool. **No place to put it.**
- **The "View as" role switch** (client-side role toggle in the user menu). Authz in DataQ is *server-side* — and there's no global "admin" role at all. I confirmed `admin` exists only as a **per-suite share permission** (`view < edit < admin < owner` in [suite_authz.py](backend/app/services/suite_authz.py#L38)), not a workspace role. CLAUDE.md also forbids reading Entra claims in route/service code. A client toggle that fakes admin can't drive real permissions, so this demo device can't be a real feature.
- **The framework-free component code + `_ds_bundle.js` / `window.DataQDesignSystem_*` / `window.Icon` globals.** The README is explicit: these are throwaway recreations; production uses real antd. You can't import the *code* (the *design* of every component is fully reproducible with antd).
- **The teal "Enterprise Monitor" palette** (the `uploads/DESIGN.md` brief + Figma direction). It contradicts the shipped indigo theme — and the design system *itself* already rejected it. Adding it would un-ship your actual brand.

## 2. Can't *yet* — no backend, not a design problem

The screens exist in the prototype, but there's nothing to wire them to:

- **Admin control centre.** Needs a list-all-suites/users/access endpoint and a *global admin* concept that doesn't exist (only per-suite `admin` does). Backend-blocked — already acknowledged in CLAUDE.md.
- **Specific Dashboard metrics with no data model:**
  - **"Avg. time to resolution"** — there's no incident/resolution model in DataQ at all. Nothing to compute it from.
  - **"Anomalies detected"** as a first-class count — `anomaly` is a *reserved* monitor kind; I confirmed v1 only **runs** `expectation` checks ([check_service.py:10](backend/app/services/check_service.py#L10), [run_service.py:70](backend/app/services/run_service.py#L70)). An anomaly KPI is ahead of the engine.
  - (The rest of the Dashboard — pass-rate, quality trends, suite performance, recent runs — *is* backed by real `metric_value`/run data, so those are fine.)
- **Workspace Settings** content. There's no settings/preferences router (confirmed — only `v1/` routers, no settings endpoint). The page can exist as a shell, but its controls would be non-functional placeholders until a settings backend exists. The prototype treats it as a placeholder too.

## 3. Not a "can't" — just deferred (for completeness)

- **Dark mode** — explicitly post-v1, but the tokens are already dark-ready. Addable whenever the team opts in; not blocked.

---

**The short version:** the only things truly *un-addable* are the **marketing site**, the **fake "View as" role switch**, the **prototype's component code/bundle**, and the **teal palette**. Everything else that's missing (Admin, two Dashboard KPIs, Settings) is **backend-gated, not impossible** — it needs an endpoint or a data model, not a redesign.

Cheap now; expensive to retrofit once dozens of endpoints exist. No new abstraction layer required, just the boundary.

77	### Snowflake & ADF (4 tasks — 4/4)
78	- [x] ✅ API: CRUD for Snowflake connections (DEV / QA / UAT), connection test endpoint — [PR 5](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/85) _(also introduced the `ConnectionAdapter` seam + registry per [ADR 0011](adr/0011-extensibility-seams-for-deferred-integrations.md), and `SecretStore.set` write-through — so PRs 6-8 are pure adapter additions)_

Enforces `(type, env)` uniqueness for orchestrator rows via a **partial unique index** `WHERE type IN ('adf','airflow')` per [#72](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/issues/72) / ADR 0004 — datasources excluded, so Snowflake stays many-per-env. CRUD/API reused unchanged: pure adapter + registry + migration addition.)_
80	- [x] ✅ Connection re-auth endpoint — refresh expired Key Vault token — `POST /connections/{id}/reauth` (`svc.reauth_connection`): rotates the credential through `SecretStore.set` **and** verifies it via the same adapter probe as `/test`, in one step (the gap PATCH+`/test` leave open). Rotation persists before the probe, so a bad new credential surfaces as 502 `connection_test_failed`; a store-write failure is 502 `connection_secret_write_failed` with the old credential untouched. 6 TestClient tests (rotate+verify ok, failed-verify-but-rotation-persists, write-fail 502, 404, secret-required 422). Type-agnostic — applies to all six connection types
81	- [x] ✅ Review `connections.secret_ref` nullability — decide based on Airflow basic-poll / unauthenticated S3 cases ([PR #41 nit](https://github.com/TheurgicDuke771/DataQ/pull/41)) — **decision: keep nullable.** It's NULL for the transient flush→secret-write window (create), for credential-less auth (managed-identity/IAM-role, W7, ADR 0010/0011), and for unauthenticated sources. v1 types are all secret-bearing, but presence is enforced in the **service layer** (`test_connection` → 502 without a credential), not the schema — so W7 credential-less modes need no later migration. Recorded as a comment on the `secret_ref` column in `db/models.py`
